lessons.title: Decision Trees: A Flowchart of Yes/No Questions
lessons.content:
You have already met models that draw lines through data. Now we tour the other famous **classical models** (the well-known machine-learning methods that are *not* neural networks). First up, the friendliest one: the **decision tree**.

**Hook.** Think of the game *20 Questions*, or the flowchart a nurse follows at a clinic: "Fever? Yes → ask the next question. No → ask a different one." Each yes/no question narrows things down until you land on an answer. A decision tree is exactly that, learned automatically from data.

**Build.** A decision tree is a flowchart of yes/no questions. Starting from the top, the tree asks a question about the data ("Does it have feathers?"), and the answer sends you down one of two branches. The next question splits the data further, and so on, **step by step**, until you reach the bottom. Those end points are called **leaves**, and each leaf gives the final answer — the **prediction** (for example, the animal's name, or "approve" versus "deny" a loan). "Training" the tree just means the computer choosing, from the data, which questions to ask and in what order so each split separates the answers as cleanly as possible.

The big selling point is that a tree is **interpretable**: you can read it like a recipe and explain *exactly* why it reached an answer ("feathers, then can't fly, so: penguin"). That matters when a human needs to trust or audit the decision.

There is one important catch. A single tree that is allowed to grow very **deep** — asking question after question — can end up with a tiny, ultra-specific leaf for almost every training example. It then **memorizes** the training data instead of learning the general pattern. That failure is called **overfitting**: the tree looks perfect on the data it studied but does poorly on new, unseen data. (Picture a student who memorized last year's exact exam answers but can't handle a fresh question.)

**Recap.** A decision tree is a learned flowchart of yes/no questions that splits the data down to leaves, where each leaf is the answer. Trees are wonderfully easy to read and explain, but a single deep tree can overfit — memorize the training data — so it stumbles on new data.

items.prompt: In plain terms, what is a decision tree?
choices: A flowchart of yes/no questions that splits the data step by step to reach an answer
A straight line drawn through scattered dots
A list of all the training examples kept in memory
A way to combine many models into one vote
items.explanation: A decision tree is a flowchart of yes/no questions; each answer sends you down a branch until you reach a leaf that gives the prediction. Drawing a straight line is regression, and combining many models is an ensemble — both are different ideas from a single tree.

items.prompt: In a decision tree, what is a 'leaf'?
choices: The very first question at the top
A training example that was thrown away
The line that separates two classes
An end point that gives the final answer (the prediction)
items.explanation: A leaf is an end point of the flowchart, where the tree stops asking questions and gives its answer. The first question sits at the top (the root), not at a leaf, and a separating line belongs to other models like SVMs.

items.prompt: Which everyday game is the closest match to how a decision tree works?
choices: Memorizing a phone number
Flipping a coin once
The game '20 Questions', where each yes/no answer narrows things down
Rolling dice and adding the totals
items.explanation: Like 20 Questions, a decision tree asks one yes/no question at a time and each answer narrows the possibilities until an answer remains. Coin flips and dice are pure chance with no narrowing questions, so they do not capture the idea.

items.prompt: Why are decision trees praised for being 'interpretable'?
choices: They run faster than every other model
They never make mistakes on new data
You can read the path of questions and explain exactly why an answer was reached
They use no data to train
items.explanation: Interpretable means you can follow the questions like a recipe and state precisely why the tree decided as it did, which builds trust. Being readable is not the same as being fast or error-free, and trees certainly do learn their questions from data.

items.prompt: What does 'training' a decision tree actually mean?
choices: Drawing the flowchart by hand with no data
The computer choosing, from the data, which questions to ask and in what order
Memorizing every answer and never asking questions
Averaging all the labels into one number
items.explanation: Training a tree is the computer learning from the data which questions split the answers most cleanly, and in what order to ask them. It is not hand-drawn, and averaging labels into a number is regression, a different task entirely.

items.prompt: What is 'overfitting'?
choices: When a model is too simple to capture any pattern
When a model runs out of memory while training
When a model memorizes the training data and then does poorly on new, unseen data
When a model predicts numbers instead of categories
items.explanation: Overfitting is memorizing the quirks of the training data so the model looks great on it but fails on fresh data. Being too simple is the opposite problem, and overfitting has nothing to do with running out of memory or with whether the output is a number.

items.prompt: Why does a single, very DEEP decision tree risk overfitting?
choices: It asks so many questions that it makes a tiny, ultra-specific leaf for almost every training example, memorizing them
It forgets the training data completely
It can only ever ask one question
Deep trees always predict the average answer
items.explanation: A very deep tree keeps splitting until nearly every training example gets its own narrow leaf, so it memorizes specifics rather than learning the general pattern. That is overfitting; a deep tree asks many questions, not one, and does not default to predicting an average.

items.prompt: A loan system needs to EXPLAIN to a regulator exactly why each applicant was approved or denied. Which property of decision trees makes them a good fit here?
choices: They are impossible for humans to read
They are interpretable — you can trace the questions and explain the decision
They never need any training data
They always beat every other model on accuracy
items.explanation: A tree's path of questions can be read aloud as the reason for the decision, which is exactly what an audit needs. Trees are readable rather than opaque, do require training data, and are not guaranteed to be the most accurate model.

items.prompt: A tree predicts 'penguin' by following: feathers? yes → can it fly? no → penguin. What did each yes/no answer do?
choices: Sent you down a branch, splitting the possibilities until a leaf gave the answer
Added two numbers together
Picked a random animal each time
Drew a straight boundary line on a graph
items.explanation: Each answer routes you down a branch of the flowchart, narrowing the options step by step until a leaf supplies the prediction. No arithmetic, randomness, or boundary line is involved — it is purely following the path of questions.

items.prompt: Which statement about decision trees is TRUE?
choices: A single tree can never overfit
Trees are easy to read and explain, but a single deep tree can overfit
Trees can only predict numbers, never categories
A tree keeps a copy of every training example and compares distances at prediction time
items.explanation: Trees are prized for being readable, yet a single deep one can memorize the training data and overfit — both halves matter. Trees can predict categories, and keeping all examples to compare distances describes k-NN, a different model.

lessons.title: k-NN and SVM: Neighbors and the Widest Gap
lessons.content:
Two more classical models, two more vivid pictures. Both are ways to sort a new example into a **class** (a category).

**Hook — k-Nearest Neighbors (k-NN).** "You're judged by the company you keep." To guess something about a new house, you might peek at the houses right next to it. That is the whole idea of **k-Nearest Neighbors**: to classify a new point, find the **k closest** known examples (k is just a small number you pick, like 5) and take a **majority vote** of their labels.

**Build k-NN.** Here is the quirk: k-NN does no real *training*. It simply **remembers all the data**, and only when a new point arrives does it measure **distances** to find the nearest neighbors and vote. So beginners who say "k-NN trains a model" are mistaken — it just stores examples and compares distances on the spot. Choosing **k** matters. Too **small** (say k = 1) and a single oddball neighbor can decide the answer, making predictions jumpy and **noisy**. Too **big** and you average over so many points that you **blur** the boundary between classes. A middling k usually works best.

**Hook — Support Vector Machine (SVM).** Now imagine two neighborhoods with an empty street between them, and you must paint the fairest dividing line. You would run it right down the **middle of the widest** part of that street.

**Build SVM.** A **Support Vector Machine** does exactly this: it places the boundary between two classes so the **margin** — the empty gap on either side — is as **wide as possible**. The boundary is pinned in place by the handful of borderline examples sitting closest to it; those critical points are the **support vectors** (hence the name). What if the classes can't be split by a straight line? SVMs use the **kernel trick** — in plain words, they cleverly *bend the space* so a curvy boundary becomes a straight one, then separate it there. No math needed; just picture warping the playing field until a straight cut does the job.

**Recap.** k-NN classifies a new point by a majority vote of its k nearest neighbors; it never really trains, it just stores the data and compares distances, and the choice of k matters (too small = noisy, too big = blurry). An SVM draws the boundary with the widest possible margin, balanced on the borderline support vectors, and the kernel trick bends the space so curvy splits become straight.

items.prompt: How does k-Nearest Neighbors classify a NEW point?
choices: It draws the widest-margin line between classes
It finds the k closest known examples and takes a majority vote of their labels
It asks a series of yes/no questions
It averages every label in the whole dataset
items.explanation: k-NN looks at the k nearest stored examples and lets them vote, assigning the new point the majority label. Drawing a widest-margin line is an SVM and asking yes/no questions is a decision tree, so neither describes k-NN.

items.prompt: A beginner says 'k-NN trains a model just like the others.' Why is this WRONG?
choices: k-NN trains for far longer than any other model
k-NN never uses the data at all
k-NN does no real training; it just remembers all the data and compares distances when a new point arrives
k-NN only works on numbers, so it cannot classify
items.explanation: k-NN skips a real training step: it stores the examples and only does work (measuring distances and voting) at prediction time. It clearly uses the data — it keeps all of it — and it can classify, so those other claims miss the point.

items.prompt: Which everyday saying best captures the spirit of k-NN?
choices: "Measure twice, cut once."
"You're judged by the company you keep."
"The early bird gets the worm."
"Don't count your chickens before they hatch."
items.explanation: k-NN labels a point by its nearest neighbors, so "you're judged by the company you keep" fits perfectly. The other sayings are about care, timing, or caution and say nothing about being classified by those nearest you.

items.prompt: What tends to go wrong if you choose k TOO SMALL (for example k = 1)?
choices: The classes get blurred together
The model refuses to make any prediction
It turns into a decision tree
A single oddball neighbor can decide the answer, making predictions jumpy and noisy
items.explanation: With a tiny k, one stray or mislabeled neighbor can swing the vote, so the predictions become noisy and unstable. Blurring the classes is the opposite failure, caused by k that is too big, not too small.

items.prompt: And if you choose k TOO BIG?
choices: Predictions become jumpy and noisy
You average over so many points that the boundary between classes gets blurred
The model can only ever predict one class
Distances no longer matter at all
items.explanation: A very large k pulls in many far-away points, smearing the line between classes so finer distinctions are lost. Noisiness is the small-k problem; with large k the issue is over-smoothing, not noise.

items.prompt: What does a Support Vector Machine (SVM) try to do?
choices: Draw the boundary between two classes with the widest possible margin (gap)
Memorize every training point and vote among neighbors
Ask yes/no questions down a flowchart
Predict an open-ended number like a price
items.explanation: An SVM positions the dividing boundary so the empty margin on each side is as wide as possible. Voting among neighbors is k-NN and yes/no questions are a tree, so those describe other models, not an SVM.

items.prompt: In an SVM, what are the 'support vectors'?
choices: The labels printed on the graph's axes
The two farthest-apart points in the data
The borderline examples closest to the boundary that pin it in place
The yes/no questions the model asks
items.explanation: Support vectors are the critical borderline points nearest the boundary; they are what hold the dividing line in position and give the method its name. They are not axis labels, the most distant points, or questions — those belong to other ideas.

items.prompt: Which everyday picture best matches an SVM's widest-margin boundary?
choices: Following a recipe's steps in order
Painting the fairest dividing line right down the middle of the widest empty street between two neighborhoods
Asking the five houses nearest you for their vote
Stacking many guesses and averaging them
items.explanation: An SVM seeks the line down the middle of the widest gap, just like the fairest dividing line in the widest part of an empty street. Asking the nearest houses is k-NN and averaging many guesses is an ensemble, so those images fit other models.

items.prompt: In plain words, what is the SVM 'kernel trick' for?
choices: It deletes the support vectors to save memory
It bends the space so a curvy boundary becomes a straight one that can be separated
It counts how many neighbors vote for each class
It makes the margin as small as possible
items.explanation: The kernel trick warps the space so that classes needing a curvy split can be separated by a straight boundary instead. It does not remove support vectors, tally neighbor votes, or shrink the margin — an SVM actually wants the margin wide.

items.prompt: Match the model to its description: 'classify a new point by a majority vote of its k closest examples.'
choices: Decision tree
Support Vector Machine
Random forest
k-Nearest Neighbors
items.explanation: Voting among the k closest examples is precisely k-Nearest Neighbors. A decision tree asks yes/no questions, an SVM draws a widest-margin line, and a random forest votes among many trees — none of which is the nearest-neighbor idea.

items.prompt: Why does scaling the features (putting them on comparable ranges) matter so much for k-NN?
choices: Because k-NN asks yes/no questions and needs clean labels
Because k-NN relies on distances, and a feature with a huge range would dominate the distance and drown out the others
Because k-NN trains for a very long time
Because k-NN draws a widest-margin boundary
items.explanation: k-NN decides by distance, so a feature measured in large units would swamp the distance calculation unless features are put on comparable scales. k-NN does not ask questions or draw margins, and it barely trains at all, so those reasons do not apply.

lessons.title: Ensembles: Wisdom of the Crowd
lessons.content:
We end the tour with the trick that often wins in practice: don't rely on one model — combine **many**.

**Hook.** Ask one person to guess the number of jellybeans in a jar and they'll be off. Average the guesses of a whole crowd and the answer is often startlingly close. That is the **wisdom of the crowd**, and in machine learning it is called an **ensemble**: combine many models so their individual mistakes tend to **cancel out**, leaving a more accurate whole.

**Build — Random Forest.** The most popular ensemble of decision trees is the **random forest**. Instead of growing one deep tree (which, recall, tends to overfit — memorize the training data), you grow **many** trees, each trained on a **random slice** of the data and features, so the trees end up different from one another. To predict, every tree gets a say and the forest takes a **majority vote**. Because the trees' errors are varied, voting cancels much of the noise: a random forest is usually **far more accurate and far less overfit than a single tree**. (Keep the names straight: one tree is *not* a forest — a forest is many trees combined by a vote.)

**Build — Boosting.** A different way to combine models is **boosting**. Here the models are built **in sequence**, and each new model focuses on fixing the **mistakes** the previous ones made — like a relay team where each member patches the last member's errors. The famous versions are **gradient boosting** and **XGBoost**. These boosted-tree methods win on a great deal of real-world **tabular** data (the rows-and-columns spreadsheets that run businesses).

**Build — No single best model.** A grounding truth ties it all together: there is **no single best model** for every problem. The practical recipe is to set a simple **baseline** (recall: a trivial predictor you must beat), **try several** of these models, **evaluate** them honestly on held-out data, and keep whatever works best. And remember — none of these are neural networks, yet these classical models still power **tons** of real-world tabular and business systems today.

**Recap.** An ensemble combines many models so their mistakes cancel — wisdom of the crowd. A random forest grows many varied trees and votes, beating a single overfit tree; boosting (gradient boosting, XGBoost) builds models in sequence, each fixing the last one's errors, and dominates much tabular data. There is no universally best model: baseline, try several, evaluate, and pick the winner.

items.prompt: What is an 'ensemble' in machine learning?
choices: A single very deep decision tree
Combining many models so their individual mistakes tend to cancel out
A model that keeps all the training data and votes among neighbors
A boundary drawn with the widest possible margin
items.explanation: An ensemble pools many models so their errors offset one another, giving a more accurate whole — the wisdom of the crowd. A single deep tree, a neighbor-voting store, and a widest-margin boundary are each just one model, not a combination.

items.prompt: What does a RANDOM FOREST do?
choices: Grows one deep tree and trusts it completely
Keeps every training point and compares distances
Grows many decision trees on random slices of the data, then takes a majority vote
Bends the space so a curvy boundary becomes straight
items.explanation: A random forest trains many varied trees on random slices of the data and features and combines them by majority vote. Trusting one deep tree, comparing distances, and bending space describe a single tree, k-NN, and an SVM kernel respectively.

items.prompt: Why is a random forest usually better than a single decision tree?
choices: A single tree is impossible to train
Voting across many varied trees cancels much of the noise, so the forest is more accurate and less overfit
A forest uses no data at all
A forest is just one tree drawn larger
items.explanation: Because the trees differ, their errors are varied and the vote averages much of the noise away, reducing overfitting and raising accuracy. A single tree can certainly be trained, and a forest is genuinely many trees, not one drawn bigger.

items.prompt: A learner claims 'a single decision tree is the same thing as a random forest.' Why is this WRONG?
choices: A forest is many different trees combined by a vote, not one tree
A forest is actually simpler than one tree
A forest contains no trees at all
A single tree always votes with itself many times
items.explanation: A random forest is a whole collection of distinct trees whose votes are combined, which is what makes it sturdier than any one tree. It is not a single tree, it is built from many trees, and it is not simpler than one tree.

items.prompt: What is the core idea of BOOSTING?
choices: Keep only the single best model and discard the rest
Train every model on the exact same data at once and average them blindly
Build models in sequence, each one fixing the previous models' mistakes
Remove the hardest examples so the model looks better
items.explanation: Boosting builds models one after another, with each new model concentrating on correcting the errors left by the earlier ones. It is sequential and error-correcting, unlike keeping a lone model or training everything in parallel with no error focus.

items.prompt: Which everyday picture best matches BOOSTING?
choices: A jury all voting at the same moment
A relay team where each member patches the previous member's errors
A single expert deciding alone
Drawing the widest line down an empty street
items.explanation: Boosting works in sequence, each model fixing what the last got wrong, just like a relay where each runner patches the prior errors. Voting all at once is more like a forest, and the lone expert and widest-street images describe other methods.

items.prompt: Gradient boosting and XGBoost are especially strong on which kind of data?
choices: Real-world tabular (rows-and-columns) data
Only handwritten signatures
Only raw audio waveforms
No data at all
items.explanation: Boosted-tree methods like gradient boosting and XGBoost win on a great deal of tabular, spreadsheet-style data that runs businesses. They are not limited to signatures or audio, and like any model they need data to work.

items.prompt: Which statement reflects the 'no single best model' idea?
choices: Always use a random forest, no matter the problem
Set a baseline, try several models, evaluate them honestly on held-out data, and keep what works best
Pick the model with the longest name
Never test a model; just trust the first one you build
items.explanation: Because no one model wins everywhere, the sound practice is to baseline, try several, evaluate fairly on data the models have not seen, and keep the best. Always forcing one model, choosing by name length, or skipping evaluation all ignore that lesson.

items.prompt: How does the 'wisdom of the crowd' explain why ensembles help?
choices: One model is always right, so the crowd is unnecessary
Crowds make every model slower and worse
The crowd just copies the single best guess
Many models' mistakes tend to cancel out, so the combined answer is more reliable than any one model's
items.explanation: Like averaging many jellybean guesses, combining many models lets their varied errors offset, yielding a more reliable answer than any single model. A lone model is not always right, and a true ensemble combines independent guesses rather than copying one.

items.prompt: What is the key DIFFERENCE between a random forest and boosting?
choices: A forest builds many trees independently and votes; boosting builds models in sequence, each fixing the last one's errors
They are exactly the same method with two names
A forest uses no trees while boosting uses only trees
A forest works in sequence while boosting votes all at once
items.explanation: A random forest grows its trees independently and combines them by vote, whereas boosting builds models one after another to correct prior mistakes. The remaining options swap or merge these ideas, getting the independent-versus-sequential distinction backwards.

items.prompt: Even though none of these classical models are neural networks, why are they still worth knowing in 2026?
choices: They have been completely replaced and are never used
They still power tons of real-world tabular and business systems
They only work as toys and never in production
They can only be used after a neural network fails
items.explanation: These classical models remain workhorses behind a great deal of real tabular and business software, so they are very much in use. They are neither obsolete, toys, nor a mere fallback to neural networks.
